questions.question: Which statement best captures the difference between a **command** and an **event** in EDA?
options: A command is a request aimed at one receiver that can reject it; an event is a past-tense fact whose producer doesn't know who consumes it
A command is always asynchronous, whereas an event is always delivered synchronously to its handler
A command may have many consumers, whereas an event must have exactly one named consumer
A command always travels over HTTP, whereas an event must always travel over a message broker
questions.explanation: The distinction is about intent and ownership of the decision: a command is imperative, directed at one receiver that can refuse it; an event is a fact that already happened with zero-to-many unknown consumers. Transport (HTTP vs broker, sync vs async) is orthogonal — the same queue can carry both.

questions.question: An event can be rejected by the receiver the same way a command can be (e.g. a validation failure).
questions.explanation: An event states a fact that already happened, so it cannot be 'rejected' — there is nothing to refuse. A command is a request and the receiver owns the outcome, so it can fail validation or refuse.

questions.question: In **event-carried state transfer (ECST)**, what is the primary benefit compared with thin event notifications?
options: It removes the runtime callback dependency on the producer because consumers keep a local copy of the data
It eliminates data duplication across the services that consume the event
It guarantees strong consistency across every consumer's local copy
It makes the event log the single source of truth for the whole system
questions.explanation: ECST puts enough data in the event that consumers keep a local read-optimized copy and never call back, removing the runtime query dependency on the producer. It actually increases duplication and is eventually consistent; making the log the source of truth describes event sourcing.

questions.question: What is the difference between a **topic** and a **queue** in messaging?
options: A topic fans out a copy to every subscription; a queue delivers each message to one competing consumer
A topic delivers each message to exactly one consumer; a queue fans out copies to all consumers
A topic guarantees global ordering of messages, whereas a queue gives no ordering at all
A topic stores messages durably on disk, whereas a queue is always held only in memory
questions.explanation: Topic = pub/sub fan-out, where each subscription receives its own copy. Queue = point-to-point, where one message is consumed by exactly one of the competing consumers. Ordering and durability are separate concerns unrelated to the topic-vs-queue distinction.

questions.question: "Effectively-once" processing is achieved by combining which two things?
options: At-least-once delivery plus idempotent consumer processing
Exactly-once broker delivery plus a distributed 2PC transaction
At-most-once delivery plus a per-consumer retry limit
Global topic ordering plus a dead-letter queue
questions.explanation: Exactly-once delivery is effectively impossible across a network, so the practical target is at-least-once delivery (never lose messages) plus idempotent processing (reprocessing is harmless), which together yield effectively-once results.

questions.question: A team complains they can never tell "where the order got stuck" in a multi-service flow, and there is no single place describing the end-to-end process. Which coordination style are they most likely using, and what is the standard mitigation?
options: Choreography — the flow is implicit/emergent, so add correlation ids and distributed tracing to follow it
Orchestration — the central coordinator hides the flow, so remove it to make the steps visible again
Choreography — switch to at-most-once delivery to cut the noise that hides the flow in the logs
Orchestration — the orchestrator is a SPOF, so the fix is to add more broker partitions
questions.explanation: Choreography spreads control across reacting services so no single place describes the flow, making 'where did it get stuck?' hard. The mitigation is correlation/causation ids and distributed tracing. Orchestration is the style that centralizes and exposes the flow, so it's not the cause here.

questions.question: A service calls `SaveChanges()` to its DB and then `bus.Publish(orderPlaced)`. Occasionally an order exists with no `OrderPlaced` event downstream. What is the anti-pattern, and what makes the transactional outbox fix it?
options: The dual-write problem; the outbox inserts the event in the same DB transaction as the business write, so one transaction is the single point of atomicity
A poison message; the outbox routes the failed publish attempts to a dead-letter queue for later retry
A race condition in the broker; the outbox enables true exactly-once delivery end-to-end across the flow
Lost updates; the outbox holds a distributed 2PC transaction spanning the DB and the broker together
questions.explanation: Two non-atomic writes (DB then broker) is the dual-write problem — a crash between them leaves them inconsistent. The outbox writes the event into an outbox table in the same DB transaction, so the single DB transaction provides atomicity. It explicitly avoids 2PC and does not give end-to-end exactly-once.

questions.question: Because the outbox relay may crash after publishing an event but before marking the row as sent, it can republish the same event — which is exactly why consumers must be idempotent.
questions.explanation: The relay gives at-least-once delivery: a crash between publish and mark-sent causes republication on restart. This duplicate delivery is precisely why downstream consumers must tolerate reprocessing idempotently.

questions.question: When building a dedup/inbox table to drop duplicate deliveries, which identifier should you key on?
options: A stable producer-assigned message id, such as the outbox row id
The broker's per-delivery id, which is unique to each delivery attempt
A hash of the entire payload, recomputed fresh on each receipt
The consumer's local timestamp at the moment it processes the message
questions.explanation: Dedup needs a key that is stable across redeliveries; the producer-assigned MessageId (such as the outbox row id) stays constant. The broker's delivery id changes on each redelivery, so deduplicating on it would let duplicates slip through.

questions.question: A consumer reacts to `OrderPlaced` by running the SQL below. After a broker redelivery some customers got double points. What is the cleanest fix that does **not** change the broker?
options: Record the processed MessageId in an inbox table and apply the increment in the same transaction, skipping if the id was already seen
Switch the broker over to at-most-once delivery so that duplicate messages can never arrive at the consumer
Move the increment outside the surrounding transaction so that it commits faster and holds no locks
Add a retry limit so that the message is dead-lettered immediately after its very first attempt
questions.explanation: `balance += 10` is not naturally idempotent, so a duplicate double-applies. Deduplicating on a stable MessageId in an inbox table — inserting the id and applying the effect in one transaction — makes replays no-ops. At-most-once risks data loss, and the other options don't address duplicate processing.

questions.question: Orders for a given customer must be processed strictly in the sequence they were placed, but the topic gives no global ordering. What is the standard approach?
options: Route all of one customer's events to the same partition key / session (e.g. `CustomerId`) so they serialize, while other customers still run in parallel
Disable parallel consumers entirely so that the whole topic is processed by a single thread
Rely on the broker's global topic ordering, which every broker guarantees by default
Use at-most-once delivery so that retries can never reorder the customer's messages
questions.explanation: Brokers order only within a partition/session, so routing by CustomerId serializes one customer's events to a single consumer while preserving cross-customer parallelism. Global topic ordering is not guaranteed, and single-threading the whole topic needlessly destroys throughput.

questions.question: Which statements about eventual consistency in EDA are correct? Select all that apply.
options: You cannot enforce a cross-service invariant synchronously; coordinate with events and compensation instead of distributed locks
There is a window where the read model lags the write and parts of the system disagree
It lets a consumer be down and catch up later from the broker's buffer
It guarantees all replicas reflect a write before the producer's transaction commits
It removes the need for idempotent consumers because messages arrive exactly once
questions.explanation: EDA cannot enforce synchronous cross-service invariants (use compensation, not distributed locks), there is a lag window where read models trail writes, and a down consumer can catch up from the buffer. It does NOT make all replicas converge before commit, and it actually demands idempotent consumers because delivery is at-least-once.

questions.question: Your Ordering context raises an internal `OrderConfirmed` domain event handled in the same transaction. A separate Marketing service now wants to react. How should you expose it?
options: Translate it into a versioned integration event (e.g. `OrderConfirmedIntegrationEvent`) published to the broker
Publish the internal `OrderConfirmed` domain event directly onto the bus as the cross-service contract
Have Marketing query the Ordering service's database directly for confirmed orders
Make `OrderConfirmed` synchronous and call Marketing inline before the transaction commits
questions.explanation: Leaking a domain event onto the bus makes internal refactors break every external consumer. Translate domain → integration at the boundary into a versioned, backward-compatible public contract delivered with eventual consistency. The domain event stays internal and free to change.

questions.question: Which situations argue **against** adopting EDA (i.e. favor a simpler synchronous design)? Select all that apply.
options: A single small app with simple CRUD
You need a synchronous, transactional answer right now
The team can't yet operate a broker or distributed tracing
Reactions are open-ended with many or unknown consumers
Spiky load needs buffering to absorb traffic
questions.explanation: EDA adds latency and moving parts that don't pay off for simple CRUD, for synchronous/transactional needs, or for a team that can't operate a broker. Open-ended consumers and spiky-load buffering are classic reasons to adopt EDA, not avoid it.

questions.question: Consider this idempotent consumer. Why is it essential that the dedup-check insert and the business effect commit in the **same** transaction?
options: Otherwise a crash could apply the business effect but not record the id (or vice versa), so a redelivery would double-apply or wrongly skip
Otherwise the broker would assign a new delivery id and break the AnyAsync check
Otherwise the consumer could not subscribe to more than one topic at a time
Otherwise EF Core cannot serialize the ProcessedMessage entity to JSON
questions.explanation: Committing the id-record and the business change atomically guarantees they happen together. If they were separate, a crash in between could leave the effect applied but the id unrecorded (causing a double-apply on redelivery) or the id recorded with no effect (causing a wrongful skip). The other options are unrelated to atomicity.

questions.question: A broker's "exactly-once" feature (e.g. Kafka EOS) guarantees that an external side effect like sending an email or charging a payment also happens exactly once.
questions.explanation: Broker exactly-once holds only inside that broker's transactional boundary — it does not cover your DB writes or external side effects (emails, payments). Across the whole flow you still need idempotency to avoid duplicate side effects.

questions.question: Comparing a **change-data-capture (CDC)** outbox relay with a **polling** relay, which characterization is correct?
options: CDC tails the transaction log to avoid polling overhead and reduce lag, while polling is simpler but adds latency and load
CDC requires a distributed 2PC transaction across the DB and broker, whereas polling avoids needing 2PC
Polling guarantees exactly-once publication of each event, whereas CDC can only manage at-least-once
CDC removes the need for any stable message id on the event, whereas polling still requires one
questions.explanation: CDC tails the DB log so it avoids polling overhead and lag; polling is simpler to build but adds latency and load. Either approach is still at-least-once and carries a stable message id for dedup; neither uses 2PC.

questions.question: A team chose thin **event notifications** (id only) to keep messages small and coupling low. What hidden downside does this specifically reintroduce that ECST avoids?
options: A runtime query dependency, because consumers must call back to the producer for details
Unbounded data duplication, because every consumer now stores a full local copy
A loss of audit history, because thin notifications cannot be replayed later
Strong consistency, because the callback forces a synchronous write path
questions.explanation: Notifications are a thin signal, so consumers must call back to the producer for details — reintroducing a runtime query dependency and callback load, and hiding causal flows. ECST avoids exactly this by carrying the data. Duplication is the cost of ECST, not notifications, and notifications don't add audit history.
